text:
Interviews have long been regarded as the cornerstone of recruitment processes, especially in large corporations. While they offer employers a chance to assess candidates’ communication skills, confidence, and cultural fit, I believe that relying solely on interviews is neither reliable nor sufficient. Although interviews hold some value, they are prone to subjectivity, bias, and inconsistency—factors that significantly undermine their effectiveness as a selection tool.

One major drawback of interviews is their susceptibility to unconscious bias. Interviewers often form snap judgments based on appearance, accent, or even body language, rather than objective evidence of competence. Research has shown that structured interviews—those with standardized questions and scoring rubrics—are more predictive of job performance than unstructured ones. Yet, many companies still conduct informal, conversational interviews, which yield highly variable results. Moreover, nervousness or social anxiety can mask a candidate’s true abilities, leading qualified individuals to be unfairly overlooked.

In contrast, alternative methods such as work-sample tests, cognitive ability assessments, and situational judgment tests provide more objective and job-relevant data. For instance, asking a software developer to solve a real coding problem reveals far more about their technical proficiency than answering hypothetical questions about teamwork. Similarly, personality and integrity tests, when used ethically and alongside other measures, can help predict reliability and ethical decision-making—traits critical in many roles.

That said, interviews should not be discarded entirely. When combined with evidence-based tools and conducted rigorously, they can complement other assessments by offering insight into interpersonal dynamics and motivation. The key lies not in abandoning interviews, but in rethinking their role: they should serve as one component of a balanced, multi-method evaluation system—not the sole gatekeeper of opportunity.

In conclusion, while interviews remain widely used, they are an unreliable standalone method for hiring. To build fairer, more effective recruitment practices, companies must integrate interviews with validated, objective assessment tools—and prioritize evidence over intuition.

Businesses always aim to make more sales, as increased revenue is essential for growth, competitiveness, and long-term sustainability. To achieve this goal, companies employ a variety of strategies—ranging from pricing tactics and promotional campaigns to digital marketing and customer relationship management.

One common method is offering discounts and limited-time promotions. These create urgency and encourage immediate purchases, especially among price-sensitive consumers. Another approach is improving product quality or adding new features, which enhances perceived value and fosters brand loyalty. Many businesses also invest heavily in advertising—through social media, search engines, or traditional channels—to increase visibility and attract new customers. Additionally, expanding distribution channels—for example, selling online in addition to physical stores—helps reach wider audiences. Perhaps most importantly, businesses focus on delivering excellent customer service; satisfied customers are more likely to return and recommend the brand to others.

Among these methods, building strong, trust-based customer relationships stands out as the best approach. While discounts may boost short-term sales, they can erode profit margins and train customers to wait for deals. In contrast, consistently meeting customer needs, personalizing interactions, and resolving issues promptly foster genuine loyalty. Loyal customers not only buy repeatedly but also act as brand advocates—sharing positive experiences organically, which is far more credible and cost-effective than paid advertising. Moreover, retaining an existing customer is significantly cheaper than acquiring a new one. Therefore, although all sales methods have merit, prioritizing long-term relationships delivers sustainable growth, stronger brand equity, and resilience in changing markets.

The rapid growth in the production of consumer goods has brought convenience and improved living standards, but it has also led to serious environmental degradation. Forests are cleared for raw materials, rivers are polluted by industrial waste, and greenhouse gas emissions from factories accelerate climate change. This troubling trend stems from several interconnected causes, and addressing it requires coordinated efforts from governments, businesses, and individuals.

One major cause is the prevailing economic model that prioritizes endless growth and short-term profit over sustainability. Companies often choose cheaper, environmentally harmful production methods to cut costs and maximize output. Another factor is rising consumer demand fueled by aggressive advertising and a culture of disposability—people buy more, use items briefly, and discard them quickly, leading to excessive resource extraction and waste. Additionally, weak environmental regulations in many countries, or poor enforcement of existing laws, allow industries to externalize environmental costs.

To tackle this problem, multiple solutions must be implemented simultaneously. Governments should introduce stricter environmental standards, impose carbon taxes, and subsidize green technologies and circular economy initiatives. Businesses need to adopt sustainable supply chains, design products for longevity and recyclability, and transparently report their ecological footprint. On an individual level, consumers can shift toward mindful consumption—choosing quality over quantity, repairing instead of replacing, and supporting eco-conscious brands. Education also plays a vital role: raising public awareness about the true cost of consumption can gradually reshape societal values.

In conclusion, environmental damage caused by consumer goods production is not inevitable—it is the result of specific choices and systems that can be reformed. With political will, corporate responsibility, and informed citizen action, we can build an economy that meets human needs without compromising the health of our planet.

Advertising is often dismissed as mere entertainment or even manipulation, especially by those who believe that a truly good product will sell itself. While it is true that quality and functionality are essential for long-term success, I strongly disagree with the claim that advertising is unnecessary. In today’s saturated and fast-paced market, advertising plays a vital, practical role—not as a substitute for quality, but as a necessary bridge between a useful product and the people who need it.

First, even excellent products remain invisible without effective communication. A groundbreaking eco-friendly water bottle may reduce plastic waste and last ten years, but if consumers have never heard of it—or worse, confuse it with dozens of similar-looking brands—they simply won’t consider purchasing it. Advertising informs people about features, benefits, availability, and context of use. It answers basic questions: What problem does this solve? How is it different? Where can I buy it? Without such information, even the most innovative solution risks obscurity.

Second, advertising shapes perception and builds trust. People do not make rational decisions in isolation; they rely on cues—brand reputation, endorsements, user reviews, and consistent messaging—all of which are cultivated through strategic advertising. For example, when a new health app highlights clinical validation and real-user testimonials in its campaigns, it doesn’t deceive—it reassures. In an era of information overload and skepticism, credible advertising helps cut through noise and signals reliability.

Finally, advertising drives competition and innovation. When companies invest in communicating value, they’re incentivized to improve not only their products but also their customer experience, sustainability practices, and ethical standards—because advertising that misleads or exaggerates quickly backfires in the age of social media and transparency.

Of course, advertising should be honest, responsible, and respectful of consumers’ intelligence. But to call it “unnecessary” overlooks the reality of human behavior, market complexity, and the sheer effort required to connect meaningful solutions with those who need them. A great product is the foundation—but advertising is the doorway. Without it, even the best ideas may never cross the threshold.

Children find it difficult to concentrate on or pay attention to their studies in school for several interconnected reasons. First, the modern digital environment plays a significant role: smartphones, social media, and online games offer constant stimulation and instant rewards, which make traditional classroom activities—often slower-paced and less interactive—seem dull by comparison. Second, many students suffer from insufficient sleep, poor nutrition, or lack of physical activity, all of which directly impair cognitive function and attention span. Third, teaching methods sometimes fail to match students’ diverse learning styles and developmental needs; lessons that are too abstract, overly long, or disconnected from real life can quickly lead to disengagement. Finally, underlying issues such as anxiety, undiagnosed learning difficulties, or an unsupportive classroom atmosphere may further hinder focus.

To address this challenge, a multi-layered approach is essential. Schools should integrate more active, student-centered learning strategies—such as project-based tasks, collaborative discussions, and short, focused activities aligned with attention spans—to rekindle interest and participation. Teachers benefit from professional development in inclusive pedagogy and classroom management techniques that foster emotional safety and routine. At home, parents can help by establishing consistent sleep schedules, limiting screen time before school, and encouraging regular exercise and balanced meals. Moreover, schools should collaborate with psychologists and counselors to identify and support children with attention-related challenges early. Ultimately, improving concentration is not about demanding more discipline from children, but about creating environments—both in and out of the classroom—that nurture their natural curiosity, well-being, and capacity to learn.

It is often argued that once a nation reaches a certain level of affluence, further economic growth no longer translates into greater happiness for its people. While I acknowledge that material wealth alone cannot guarantee lasting satisfaction, I disagree with the claim that additional economic growth has no positive impact on citizens’ well-being in already wealthy countries.

To begin with, higher national income can fund public goods and services that directly enhance quality of life—such as universal healthcare, high-quality education, affordable housing, and sustainable infrastructure. For example, Scandinavian countries like Norway and Denmark, despite being among the world’s wealthiest nations, continue to invest heavily in social welfare and environmental protection, contributing significantly to their consistently high rankings in global happiness reports. These investments reflect how economic resources, even beyond basic needs, enable societies to address complex challenges like mental health, inequality, and climate change—issues that profoundly affect citizen satisfaction.

Moreover, economic growth fosters innovation and opportunity. In affluent societies, rising prosperity supports research in medicine, clean energy, and digital accessibility—improving longevity, reducing daily stress, and expanding personal freedoms. A well-funded public transport system, for instance, may reduce commuting time and pollution, thereby increasing life satisfaction more meaningfully than an extra percentage point in GDP alone suggests.

That said, it is true that beyond a certain threshold, income gains yield diminishing returns on subjective well-being. Studies show that once individuals meet fundamental needs—food, shelter, security—factors like social connection, purposeful work, and autonomy become stronger predictors of happiness than higher salaries. Yet this does not mean economic growth is irrelevant; rather, it highlights the need for *how* wealth is distributed and spent. Unequal growth may widen social divides and erode trust, while inclusive, values-driven growth can strengthen community resilience and individual fulfillment.

In conclusion, while money cannot buy happiness outright, sustained and thoughtful economic development remains vital—even in rich countries—for building the conditions in which people can thrive. The key lies not in rejecting growth, but in redefining progress to prioritize human dignity, sustainability, and shared prosperity.

Many school leavers today choose to take a gap year—travelling, volunteering, or working—before starting university. This trend has grown significantly, and while it offers valuable opportunities for personal growth and real-world experience, it also presents certain challenges.

One major advantage is the development of maturity and independence. Living away from home, managing finances, navigating unfamiliar cultures, or handling workplace responsibilities helps young people build confidence and practical life skills that textbooks cannot teach. For example, someone who works in customer service for a year may improve their communication and problem-solving abilities—skills highly valued in both academic and professional settings. Moreover, travel broadens perspectives: encountering different values, languages, and social systems fosters empathy and global awareness, which enriches university studies, especially in subjects like international relations, anthropology, or literature.

Another benefit is increased clarity about future goals. Some students enter university unsure of their interests or career path. A gap year allows them time to reflect, explore options, and gain firsthand insight into potential fields—perhaps through internships or part-time work—leading to more informed and motivated study choices. Research shows that students who take structured gap years often perform better academically and are more engaged on campus.

However, there are notable disadvantages. Financial constraints can limit access to meaningful gap-year experiences, making this option less equitable. There is also the risk of losing academic momentum: after a year away from formal study, some students struggle to readjust to disciplined learning routines or feel disconnected from peers who started university immediately. In addition, without clear planning, a gap year can become unstructured and unproductive—simply delaying decisions rather than enhancing them. Finally, in competitive fields or countries where early specialization is expected, taking time off might be viewed negatively by admissions officers or employers, though this perception is gradually changing.

In conclusion, a well-planned gap year can be a transformative experience, offering personal, cultural, and professional benefits that complement formal education. Yet its success depends heavily on intentionality, preparation, and individual circumstances. Rather than seeing it as a break from learning, it should be regarded as an alternative, equally valuable form of education—one that equips young people not just with knowledge, but with wisdom.

Pollution and environmental degradation are often seen as inevitable byproducts of economic growth, especially in developing nations striving to improve living standards. While it is true that industrialization and urbanization historically correlate with increased emissions, resource depletion, and habitat loss, I disagree that such damage is unavoidable. With proactive policies, technological innovation, and a shift in development priorities, countries can pursue prosperity without sacrificing ecological integrity.

Historically, many high-income countries experienced severe environmental decline during their industrial revolutions—think of London’s “Great Smog” of 1952 or the Cuyahoga River catching fire in Ohio. At first glance, this suggests a linear trade-off: growth equals pollution. However, this view overlooks the concept of the Environmental Kuznets Curve, which posits that environmental quality initially deteriorates with income but eventually improves after a certain threshold—provided governments invest in cleaner technologies, enforce regulations, and prioritize sustainability. South Korea, for instance, transformed from a heavily polluted industrializer in the 1980s to a global leader in green energy investment and urban reforestation by the 2010s.

Moreover, today’s developing nations have access to knowledge, tools, and international support unavailable to earlier industrializers. Renewable energy costs have plummeted; solar power is now cheaper than coal in most parts of the world. Countries like Costa Rica generate over 98% of their electricity from renewables, proving that economic development and environmental stewardship can go hand in hand. Crucially, avoiding environmental damage is not just ethically sound—it is economically rational. Air pollution alone costs the global economy over $8 trillion annually in health care and lost productivity. Investing early in sustainable infrastructure prevents far costlier remediation later.

Of course, challenges remain—weak governance, short-term political incentives, and unequal access to green technology can hinder progress. But these are institutional and policy failures, not natural laws. The real obstacle is not feasibility, but will.

In conclusion, while rapid development has often led to environmental harm, it is inaccurate—and dangerously defeatist—to claim this outcome is unavoidable. With foresight, commitment, and global cooperation, wealth and well-being need not come at the planet’s expense. The choice is not between growth and greenness, but between smart growth and reckless growth.

In recent years, rapid urbanization has led to severe accommodation shortages and chronic transport congestion in many large cities. As a response, governments and planners have begun encouraging businesses to relocate to rural areas. While this strategy offers several promising benefits, it also presents notable challenges. Overall, the advantages do outweigh the disadvantages—but only if implemented thoughtfully and with strong support mechanisms.

One major advantage is the alleviation of urban pressure. When companies move out, demand for housing and office space in cities decreases, potentially stabilizing rents and easing overcrowded public transport systems. Simultaneously, rural areas gain economic vitality: new jobs are created, local infrastructure improves, and young people are less likely to migrate to cities in search of opportunity. This redistribution can foster more balanced regional development and reduce the stark urban-rural divide.

Moreover, relocating businesses often encourages greener practices. Rural locations typically offer more space for sustainable design—such as solar panels, rainwater harvesting, or eco-friendly commuting options—while reducing the carbon footprint associated with dense, energy-intensive city operations.

However, significant drawbacks exist. Many rural areas lack reliable high-speed internet, skilled labor pools, or efficient logistics networks—essential for modern enterprises. Employees may resist relocation due to concerns about education, healthcare, or cultural amenities. There is also a risk that poorly planned migration could harm fragile rural ecosystems or inflate local living costs beyond residents’ means.

Crucially, these disadvantages are not inevitable. With targeted investment in digital connectivity, vocational training, and public services—and with incentives for both businesses and workers—the transition can be smooth and mutually beneficial.

In conclusion, shifting businesses to rural areas is a pragmatic and forward-looking solution to urban strain. Though challenges remain, they are manageable through coordinated policy and long-term commitment. The potential gains—in sustainability, equity, and resilience—make this strategy not just viable, but necessary for inclusive national progress.

In today’s fast-paced digital world, people are increasingly dependent on the Internet and smartphones. From checking emails and navigating cities to ordering food and staying in touch with loved ones, these technologies have become indispensable tools in daily life. While this dependence brings undeniable convenience and efficiency, it also raises serious concerns about mental health, social skills, and personal autonomy. In my view, although the benefits are significant, they do not fully outweigh the disadvantages—especially when usage becomes excessive and unreflective.

On the positive side, the Internet and smartphones have revolutionized access to information and communication. Students can attend online classes, researchers can access global academic databases instantly, and remote workers can collaborate across continents in real time. Emergency services, health apps, and digital banking have improved safety and financial inclusion. Moreover, for people living far from family or with mobility challenges, video calls and messaging apps provide vital emotional support and social connection.

However, the growing dependence carries substantial risks. Many users experience digital fatigue, anxiety, and disrupted sleep due to constant notifications and screen exposure. Studies link heavy smartphone use to reduced attention spans, especially among adolescents, and declining face-to-face communication skills. Social media, while connecting people globally, often fosters comparison, loneliness, and even addiction—replacing meaningful interaction with superficial engagement. Furthermore, overreliance on GPS navigation weakens spatial memory; dependence on search engines diminishes critical thinking and retention; and automatic spell-checks erode basic writing competence.

What makes the imbalance more troubling is that the disadvantages tend to accumulate silently over time, while benefits are immediate and visible. A person may celebrate saving ten minutes by using a ride-hailing app, yet overlook how years of avoiding small talk with strangers have made them uncomfortable in real-world conversations.

In conclusion, the Internet and smartphones are powerful tools that enrich our lives—but only when used intentionally and moderately. The advantages are real and transformative, yet the psychological, cognitive, and social costs of unchecked dependence are too profound to ignore. Rather than asking whether benefits outweigh drawbacks, we should ask how we can harness technology wisely—preserving human connection, mental well-being, and independent thought as our highest priorities.

Mobile phones and the internet offer numerous benefits for older people, yet this age group remains the least likely to use them. Bridging this digital divide is both meaningful and urgent.

Firstly, technology enhances communication and combats loneliness—a major concern among the elderly. With video calls, messaging apps, and social media, seniors can stay in regular contact with family members, especially those living far away. A grandparent can watch a grandchild’s first steps in real time or share daily moments with loved ones, strengthening emotional bonds. Secondly, mobile devices provide easy access to vital information and services: from online health consultations and medication reminders to digital banking and public transport updates. This independence supports safer, more confident daily living. Thirdly, the internet offers rich opportunities for lifelong learning and mental stimulation—online courses, language apps, puzzles, and virtual museum tours help keep the mind active and engaged.

To encourage older people to embrace these tools, we must adopt patient, person-centered approaches. Digital literacy programs designed specifically for seniors—offered in community centers, libraries, or care homes—can build confidence step by step. Trainers should avoid jargon, use large-print guides, and allow plenty of practice time. Family involvement is also key: grandchildren or younger relatives can serve as supportive, non-judgmental tech mentors during relaxed, one-on-one sessions. Moreover, manufacturers and app developers should prioritize accessibility—larger icons, voice commands, simplified interfaces, and clear instructions make devices far more usable. Finally, highlighting real-life success stories—such as an 80-year-old who reconnected with childhood friends online or started a blog about gardening—can inspire motivation through relatable, positive examples.

In conclusion, mobile phones and the internet hold transformative potential for older adults’ well-being, autonomy, and connection. With empathy, tailored support, and inclusive design, we can empower them not just to use technology—but to thrive with it.

Nowadays more and more people want to get things done instantly—whether it’s ordering food, receiving news, completing a transaction, or solving a problem. This growing demand for immediacy is largely driven by rapid technological advancement, especially the widespread use of smartphones and high-speed internet. With just a few taps, we can access vast amounts of information, connect with others across the globe, or have goods delivered within hours. Social media and on-demand platforms have further conditioned us to expect instant responses and gratification. Additionally, modern lifestyles—characterized by packed schedules and heightened competition—have made efficiency and speed not just convenient but essential for many.

However, while instantaneity brings undeniable benefits, such as increased productivity and convenience, it also carries significant drawbacks. The constant expectation of immediacy can lead to impatience, reduced attention spans, and chronic stress. People may struggle to tolerate delays—even minor ones—leading to frustration in personal relationships or professional settings. Moreover, the pursuit of speed often compromises depth: quick answers replace thoughtful reflection; fast content replaces meaningful learning; and automated services erode human interaction and empathy. In education and creative fields, for instance, true understanding and innovation still require time, trial, and quiet contemplation—qualities that instant culture tends to undermine.

In my view, this trend is neither wholly positive nor entirely negative—it is a double-edged sword. What matters most is our ability to recognize when speed serves us and when it hinders us. Cultivating mindfulness, setting boundaries around technology use, and consciously choosing slowness in certain areas—like reading, listening, or building relationships—can help restore balance. Ultimately, progress should not be measured only by how fast we move, but by how wisely and humanely we live.

Restoring old buildings in major cities does require significant government funding, and it is tempting to argue that such resources would be better spent on constructing new housing and roads—urgent needs in rapidly growing urban areas. However, I strongly disagree with the view that restoration should be abandoned in favor of purely functional infrastructure. While improving living conditions and transportation is vital, preserving historic architecture offers irreplaceable cultural, economic, and social benefits that cannot be achieved through new construction alone.

Firstly, old buildings are tangible links to a nation’s identity and collective memory. A cathedral, a colonial-era market, or a centuries-old university building tells stories of craftsmanship, resilience, and societal evolution. Once demolished, that heritage is lost forever—not just as stone and timber, but as context for understanding who we are. New houses and roads, no matter how well-designed, cannot replicate this depth of meaning. Moreover, thoughtful restoration need not exclude modern utility; many historic districts successfully integrate energy-efficient systems, accessible facilities, and mixed-use spaces—proving that preservation and progress are not mutually exclusive.

Secondly, heritage conservation stimulates sustainable economic growth. Restored historic centres attract tourism, foster local crafts and small businesses, and increase property values—not through speculation, but through authentic cultural appeal. In contrast, poorly planned mass construction can lead to urban sprawl, increased traffic congestion, and social segregation. Well-maintained historic areas often encourage walkability and community interaction, indirectly supporting public health and reducing long-term infrastructure costs.

Finally, the argument assumes a false dichotomy: that funds must go *either* to restoration *or* to housing and roads. In reality, smart urban policy allocates budgets across multiple priorities. Governments can adopt phased restoration, leverage private partnerships, and prioritize sites with high cultural significance and adaptive reuse potential—freeing up resources for other needs without sacrificing heritage.

In conclusion, while expanding housing and upgrading transport networks remain critical, diverting all heritage funds toward such projects would impoverish cities spiritually and economically. Preserving old buildings is not an indulgence—it is an investment in continuity, sustainability, and shared humanity. The most vibrant cities are not those that erase the past to build the future, but those that weave both into a coherent, living whole.

Climate change is one of the most pressing challenges of our time, and opinions differ sharply on how humanity should respond. While some argue that prevention is no longer feasible and that we must instead focus on adaptation—learning to live with rising temperatures, extreme weather, and shifting ecosystems—I firmly believe that mitigation remains both essential and achievable. To abandon prevention in favor of mere adaptation is not only dangerously shortsighted but also unjust, especially to vulnerable communities who contributed least to the crisis.

Adaptation measures—such as building sea walls, developing drought-resistant crops, or relocating coastal populations—are undoubtedly necessary and already underway in many parts of the world. However, they address symptoms rather than causes. Relying solely on adaptation assumes that all societies have equal resources, knowledge, and political stability to adjust—a false assumption. Small island nations, for instance, cannot “live with” sea-level rise that submerges their entire territory. Similarly, millions of subsistence farmers in sub-Saharan Africa lack the infrastructure or funding to switch crops or install irrigation systems. Adaptation without mitigation deepens global inequality and risks normalizing irreversible ecological damage.

Moreover, scientific evidence continues to affirm that aggressive mitigation—cutting greenhouse gas emissions, transitioning to renewable energy, protecting forests, and rethinking consumption patterns—can still limit warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) stresses that every fraction of a degree matters: limiting warming to 1.5°C instead of 2°C could save hundreds of millions of people from climate-related risks and preserve vital ecosystems like coral reefs. Prevention is not futile; it is urgent, technically possible, and economically prudent in the long term—far less costly than decades of escalating disaster response and displacement.

That said, framing mitigation and adaptation as mutually exclusive is misleading. A responsible strategy must integrate both: reducing emissions *while* strengthening resilience. But prioritizing adaptation over mitigation reflects resignation—not realism. It surrenders agency, undermines intergenerational justice, and ignores the moral imperative to protect the planet we share.

In conclusion, we must refuse the false choice between prevention and adaptation. Living *with* climate change should never mean accepting its worst consequences as inevitable. Instead, we must act decisively to prevent further harm—because the future we hope to adapt to depends entirely on the choices we make today.

In today’s rapidly evolving society, the allocation of public funds remains a subject of intense debate. The suggestion that money should be spent on constructing new public buildings—such as museums or town halls—rather than renovating existing ones raises important questions about heritage, functionality, sustainability, and civic responsibility. While new buildings can symbolize progress and attract investment, I largely disagree with the idea that renovation should be deprioritized, because preserving and upgrading existing infrastructure is often more practical, economical, and environmentally responsible.

Firstly, many existing public buildings possess historical, architectural, or cultural value that cannot be replicated. A century-old town hall may embody the identity of a community, serving not only as an administrative center but also as a living monument to local history. Demolishing such structures to make way for modern replacements risks erasing collective memory and weakening community cohesion. Renovation, by contrast, allows these buildings to retain their character while incorporating contemporary amenities—energy-efficient systems, accessible entrances, or digital infrastructure—ensuring they remain functional and inclusive for future generations.

Secondly, renovation is frequently more cost-effective and sustainable than new construction. Building from scratch demands vast resources: raw materials, transportation energy, labor, and time. It also generates significant waste and carbon emissions. In contrast, retrofitting an existing building reduces environmental impact, aligns with global climate goals, and often delivers faster results. For instance, upgrading insulation, installing solar panels, or modernizing heating systems in an old museum can dramatically improve its efficiency at a fraction of the cost—and carbon footprint—of erecting a new one.

That said, there are valid cases where new construction is justified—such as in rapidly growing urban areas lacking adequate civic infrastructure, or where existing buildings are structurally unsound beyond repair. However, these exceptions should be determined through careful assessment—not assumed as the default. Prioritizing new builds over renovation reflects a short-term mindset that undervalues continuity, resilience, and stewardship.

In conclusion, while new public buildings have their place, they should not come at the expense of thoughtful, respectful renovation. Public funds are finite, and our duty is to use them wisely—not just to impress, but to endure. Investing in the renewal of existing structures honors the past, serves the present, and sustains the future far more meaningfully than chasing novelty alone.

Space exploration has long been a source of fascination and scientific progress, yet it is increasingly criticized for its high financial cost. Some argue that the vast sums invested in missions to Mars or the Moon could be better spent addressing urgent problems on Earth—such as poverty, climate change, and public health. While I acknowledge the gravity of these challenges, I firmly believe that funding space exploration is not only justified but essential for humanity’s long-term survival and advancement.

Firstly, space technology yields countless practical benefits that directly improve life on Earth. Satellite systems developed for navigation and communication now support global weather forecasting, disaster management, and precision agriculture—saving lives and boosting food security. Medical innovations like digital imaging and portable ultrasound devices trace their origins to NASA-funded research. Even everyday materials such as memory foam and water-purification systems were refined through space programs. These spin-off technologies demonstrate that investment in space is rarely isolated; it catalyzes broad-based innovation across industries.

Secondly, space exploration fosters international cooperation and inspires future generations. Projects like the International Space Station bring together scientists from over a dozen countries, transcending political tensions through shared scientific goals. Moreover, images of Earth from orbit have profoundly shaped environmental awareness, while missions like Artemis or the James Webb Space Telescope ignite curiosity in young people, encouraging them to pursue careers in science, engineering, and mathematics—fields critical to solving global challenges.

Critics rightly point out that pressing issues demand immediate resources—but this is not an either/or choice. Global spending on space amounts to less than 0.1% of total government expenditures in most advanced economies, far less than subsidies for fossil fuels or military budgets. Rather than cutting space funding, we should prioritize efficient allocation and increase overall investment in science and development.

In conclusion, dismissing space exploration as a luxury ignores its tangible contributions to human welfare, technological progress, and global collaboration. Far from diverting resources, it multiplies them—turning cosmic ambition into earthly benefit. As we confront increasingly complex challenges on our own planet, the perspective, tools, and unity gained from looking beyond it may prove indispensable.

Taxation is often viewed as a necessary evil—unpopular yet indispensable. While some argue that individuals should retain all their earnings without state interference, I firmly disagree. Taxes are not merely a financial obligation; they are the lifeblood of a functioning society, funding essential services and promoting collective well-being.

Firstly, public infrastructure and services depend entirely on tax revenue. Roads, bridges, public transportation, schools, hospitals, and emergency services—all require substantial, sustained investment. Without taxation, these would either collapse or become prohibitively expensive for ordinary citizens. Imagine a world where only those who could afford private healthcare received treatment, or where children’s education depended solely on family income. Such inequality would deepen social divisions and undermine national progress.

Secondly, progressive taxation helps reduce economic disparity. When higher earners contribute proportionally more, governments can redistribute resources through welfare programs, unemployment benefits, and subsidized housing. This does not punish success but fosters stability and opportunity. A society where wealth accumulates exclusively at the top risks unrest, diminished social mobility, and weakened consumer demand—harming the economy as a whole.

Admittedly, tax evasion and inefficiency in government spending are legitimate concerns. However, these issues call for reform—not abolition. Transparency, accountability, and civic engagement can improve how taxes are collected and used. Moreover, paying tax reflects civic responsibility: just as citizens benefit from laws, security, and shared institutions, they must contribute to their maintenance.

In conclusion, while the desire to keep one’s full income is understandable, it overlooks the interdependent nature of modern society. Taxes enable fairness, safety, and opportunity. Rather than resisting taxation, we should strive to make it fairer, more efficient, and more visible in its impact. After all, what we pay is not just money—it is our shared investment in a better future for everyone.

In today’s saturated media landscape, consumers are constantly bombarded with advertisements—from television commercials and social media posts to sponsored content and targeted pop-ups. This relentless exposure raises an important question: to what extent are consumers actually influenced by advertising? While individuals vary in their susceptibility, evidence suggests that advertisements exert a significant, often subconscious, influence on consumer behavior—especially when they are emotionally resonant, repetitive, or tailored through data-driven algorithms.

Adverts shape preferences and purchasing decisions in multiple ways. First, repeated exposure fosters familiarity and perceived credibility—a psychological phenomenon known as the mere-exposure effect. Second, persuasive techniques such as celebrity endorsements, aspirational imagery, or fear-based messaging tap into deep-seated emotions and social desires, bypassing rational evaluation. Third, personalized ads, powered by online tracking, create an illusion of relevance, making recommendations feel intuitive rather than manipulative. Studies have shown that even skeptical or well-informed consumers frequently underestimate how much ads affect their choices—particularly in categories like food, fashion, and technology.

However, this influence is not inevitable or absolute. Consumers retain agency, especially when equipped with critical awareness and supportive regulatory frameworks. To protect them, a multi-layered approach is essential. Governments should enforce stricter transparency rules—requiring clear labeling of sponsored content, disclosing data collection practices, and banning deceptive claims, particularly in advertising aimed at children. Educational institutions must integrate media literacy into curricula, teaching students how to deconstruct ads, recognize bias, and evaluate sources critically. Moreover, tech platforms should offer users meaningful control over ad targeting—not just opt-out options, but intuitive, default-privacy settings and accessible explanations of how algorithms work.

Ultimately, while advertisements will always play a role in a market economy, their power need not come at the expense of consumer autonomy. By combining regulation, education, and ethical innovation, society can foster a more informed, resilient, and empowered public—one that engages with advertising thoughtfully, rather than passively.

In recent years, governments around the world have invested enormous sums in elite athletes to compete in global events such as the Olympics and World Championships. While national pride and international prestige are undeniably important, I firmly believe that redirecting a significant portion of this funding toward grassroots sports for children would yield far greater long-term benefits for society.

Supporters of heavy investment in elite competitors often argue that success on the world stage inspires national unity, boosts tourism, and attracts foreign investment. Winning medals can indeed elevate a country’s global image and motivate citizens. However, these benefits tend to be short-lived and superficial. Moreover, the vast majority of funding goes to a tiny fraction of already privileged athletes—often those with access to private coaching, advanced facilities, and years of specialized training—while millions of children lack even basic opportunities to play sports safely and regularly.

By contrast, investing in children’s sports addresses fundamental social challenges. Regular physical activity helps combat childhood obesity, improves mental health, enhances concentration in school, and fosters teamwork and discipline. When schools are equipped with proper sports equipment, trained PE teachers, and inclusive after-school programs, every child—not just the naturally gifted—can benefit. Such investment also nurtures future talent organically: many elite athletes begin not in high-performance centers, but on school playgrounds or local community fields. Without early exposure and encouragement, potential champions may never discover their abilities—or worse, drop out of physical activity entirely due to lack of access or motivation.

Of course, this is not an argument for eliminating support for elite sport altogether. A balanced approach is essential: maintaining targeted, efficient programs for top performers while significantly increasing equitable, nationwide investment in youth participation. Governments should view sports not merely as a vehicle for medals, but as a cornerstone of public health and education.

In conclusion, while supporting elite competitors has its merits, prioritizing children’s access to sports is a more ethical, sustainable, and socially transformative use of public funds. True sporting excellence begins not on the podium—but on the field where every child is given the chance to run, jump, try, and grow.

In modern societies, the question of who should bear the responsibility for supporting the elderly—governments or individuals—has sparked widespread debate. While some argue that the state must ensure financial security and care for retirees, others maintain that personal preparation through lifelong saving is both necessary and fair. Both perspectives have merit, and a balanced understanding reveals the complexity of aging populations and social responsibility.

Supporters of government responsibility emphasize fairness, solidarity, and practical necessity. They point out that not everyone has equal opportunities to save: low-income workers, those with health issues, or people in unstable employment may struggle to accumulate sufficient retirement funds. Public pension systems and state-funded healthcare act as safety nets, preventing poverty and social exclusion among the elderly. Moreover, an aging population places growing pressure on families; without state support, many adult children would face unsustainable caregiving and financial burdens. In countries like Sweden or Canada, robust public retirement programs have contributed to high levels of elder well-being and social cohesion.

On the other hand, advocates of individual responsibility stress personal accountability, economic sustainability, and efficiency. They argue that relying too heavily on government support encourages dependency and may lead to fiscal strain, especially as life expectancy rises and birth rates fall. Tax-funded pensions risk becoming unaffordable, potentially burdening future generations. Furthermore, individuals who plan ahead—through savings, investments, or private insurance—often enjoy greater autonomy and flexibility in retirement. This view also highlights that personal preparation fosters financial literacy and long-term thinking, valuable traits for any citizen.

In my opinion, the most effective approach lies not in choosing one side over the other, but in combining both responsibilities. Governments should guarantee a basic level of protection—ensuring dignity, access to essential healthcare, and a minimum pension—especially for the most vulnerable. At the same time, individuals must be empowered and encouraged to save, supported by policies such as tax incentives, financial education, and accessible retirement planning tools. A shared responsibility model promotes both social justice and economic resilience.

Ultimately, caring for the elderly reflects a society’s values. It is neither realistic nor ethical to expect individuals alone to shoulder the full weight of aging, nor is it sustainable for governments to bear it entirely. Through cooperation, foresight, and compassion, we can build systems that honor our elders while securing a stable future for all.

The debate over how government funding for research should be allocated is both timely and consequential. With limited financial resources available for post-graduation research, some argue that public money ought to be reserved exclusively for scientific endeavors—those yielding tangible innovations, medical breakthroughs, or technological advances. While this perspective reflects understandable concerns about efficiency and societal return on investment, it overlooks the indispensable role of humanities, social sciences, and arts in shaping a just, reflective, and resilient society.

Science undoubtedly drives progress: vaccines save lives, renewable energy technologies combat climate change, and AI systems transform industries. Yet science alone cannot determine *what* we ought to build, *why* we pursue certain goals, or *how* to distribute benefits fairly. These are ethical, historical, and cultural questions—precisely the domain of philosophy, history, sociology, literature, and education research. For instance, studies on algorithmic bias rely heavily on critical race theory and feminist epistemology; effective public health campaigns depend on anthropological insights into community trust and behavioral psychology. Without such research, even the most advanced scientific solutions risk being misapplied, misunderstood, or unjustly imposed.

Moreover, labeling certain fields as “less useful” reveals a narrow, instrumental view of knowledge—one that equates utility with immediate economic output. But societies thrive not only on GDP growth but also on informed citizenship, democratic participation, mental well-being, and intercultural understanding—all nurtured by non-scientific scholarship. When governments defund research into inequality, migration narratives, or language preservation, they silence vital perspectives needed to address complex, human-centered challenges.

Rather than choosing between science and the rest, policymakers should adopt a balanced, interdisciplinary approach—recognizing that real-world problems, from pandemic response to urban planning, demand collaboration across disciplines. Funding mechanisms can prioritize relevance and impact without privileging one epistemology over another. Transparency, peer review, and public engagement—not disciplinary boundaries—should guide allocation decisions.

In conclusion, limiting research funding solely to the sciences is not pragmatic—it is impoverished. A thriving society requires not only the ability to innovate but also the wisdom to question, the empathy to include, and the imagination to envision better futures. Investing in the full spectrum of human inquiry is not a luxury; it is the foundation of sustainable, equitable progress.

Some people believe that watching television is entirely harmful to children, while others argue that it can have meaningful benefits for their development. Both perspectives hold merit, and a balanced understanding is essential.

On the one hand, critics of children’s TV viewing point to several serious concerns. Excessive screen time often replaces physical activity, contributing to sedentary lifestyles and rising childhood obesity. It may also interfere with sleep patterns, especially when screens are used before bedtime, as blue light suppresses melatonin production. Moreover, not all content is age-appropriate—violent, commercialized, or overly stimulating programs can negatively influence behavior, attention spans, and emotional regulation. Studies have linked heavy early TV exposure to delayed language development and poorer academic performance, particularly when viewing replaces interactive play or conversation with caregivers.

On the other hand, television, when carefully selected and co-viewed, can support learning and growth. High-quality educational programs—such as those promoting literacy, numeracy, empathy, or scientific curiosity—have been shown to enhance vocabulary, social understanding, and cultural awareness in young viewers. For children in under-resourced communities, TV can serve as a valuable window into diverse experiences, ideas, and languages. Additionally, shared viewing offers opportunities for family discussion, critical thinking, and bonding—turning passive watching into an active, reflective experience.

In my view, television itself is neither good nor bad; its impact depends entirely on how it is used. The key lies in intentionality: choosing thoughtful content, limiting duration, encouraging interaction, and prioritizing real-world experiences. Rather than banning TV outright or allowing unrestricted access, parents and educators should guide children toward becoming discerning, engaged media users. With thoughtful boundaries and supportive engagement, television can indeed be a tool for growth—not a barrier to it.

Nowadays, celebrities are more famous for their glamour and wealth than for their achievements, and this sets a bad example to young people. I strongly agree with this statement, as the current media landscape increasingly prioritizes superficial qualities over substance, shaping young people’s values in troubling ways.

Firstly, mainstream media and social platforms constantly spotlight celebrities’ luxurious lifestyles—designer outfits, exotic vacations, and extravagant parties—while downplaying or ignoring their actual contributions. A singer known primarily for viral dance challenges may amass millions of followers, whereas a scientist developing life-saving vaccines receives far less attention. This imbalance sends a clear message: fame is attainable through image and consumption, not hard work, expertise, or social impact.

Secondly, young people, who are still forming their identities and aspirations, are especially vulnerable to such messaging. When role models are chosen for how they look or what they own rather than what they do or stand for, it distorts their understanding of success. Studies have shown that adolescents exposed to idealized celebrity content report higher levels of body dissatisfaction, materialism, and diminished motivation toward academic or civic goals. They begin to equate self-worth with visibility and wealth, rather than integrity, curiosity, or compassion.

That said, it would be unfair to claim all celebrities lack merit or influence. Many use their platform responsibly—advocating for climate action, mental health awareness, or education reform. Yet these voices often struggle to compete with sensationalized narratives. The problem lies not in celebrity culture itself, but in how fame is defined and rewarded by industry gatekeepers and audience habits.

In conclusion, when glamour and wealth overshadow genuine achievement in defining celebrity status, society fails its youth. To counter this, schools, families, and media producers must actively highlight diverse, values-driven role models—and teach critical media literacy so young people can question, reflect, and choose inspiration wisely. True fame should illuminate effort, empathy, and excellence—not just glitter.

In modern societies, the issue of financial support for retired people has sparked considerable debate. Some argue that governments bear a moral and practical responsibility to provide financial assistance to the elderly, while others maintain that individuals should plan and save for their own retirement. Both perspectives have merit, and a balanced understanding is essential.

Supporters of government-funded retirement care emphasize the social contract between citizens and the state. They point out that many older adults have contributed taxes and labor throughout their working lives, thus earning the right to security in old age. Moreover, not everyone has equal opportunities to save—low-income workers, those with health issues, or people who faced economic downturns may find it impossible to accumulate sufficient pensions. In addition, an aging population often leads to increased healthcare costs and reduced family-based care due to smaller household sizes and geographic mobility, making state intervention both necessary and compassionate.

On the other hand, advocates of self-reliance warn that overdependence on government support can discourage personal responsibility and long-term financial planning. They argue that mandatory savings schemes, such as individual pension accounts, encourage discipline and reduce future fiscal pressure on public budgets. Furthermore, excessive state involvement may lead to inefficiency, bureaucracy, or unsustainable debt—especially in countries facing demographic challenges like declining birth rates and rising life expectancy.

In my view, a hybrid approach is most reasonable. Governments should guarantee a basic safety net—such as a universal pension or subsidized healthcare—to protect vulnerable retirees, while simultaneously promoting financial literacy and incentivizing personal savings through tax benefits or employer-matched contributions. This dual strategy respects individual agency without abandoning collective responsibility. After all, caring for the elderly reflects not only economic policy but also the values of a just and humane society.

In some countries, particularly across Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia, the proportion of children aged 15 and younger continues to rise significantly. While a youthful population is often seen as a sign of vitality and potential, this demographic trend carries complex implications—both promising and challenging—for the present and future.

Currently, a large youth cohort places immense pressure on public services. Schools struggle to accommodate growing numbers of students, often resulting in overcrowded classrooms, shortages of qualified teachers, and limited access to quality education. Healthcare systems face similar strains, especially in maternal and child health services. Moreover, families with many young dependents may find it harder to escape poverty, as household resources are stretched thin and parents—especially mothers—may have less opportunity to pursue education or employment.

Looking ahead, the long-term effects hinge largely on how well these countries invest in their young people. If governments prioritize education, vocational training, healthcare, and job creation, this “youth bulge” could transform into a powerful demographic dividend: a generation of skilled, healthy, and productive workers driving economic growth and innovation. However, without such investment, the same bulge risks becoming a demographic burden—leading to high unemployment, social frustration, political instability, and increased migration pressures.

It is also worth noting that rapid population growth among the young often reflects broader issues: limited access to family planning, gender inequality, and low levels of female education. Addressing these root causes is essential—not to curb youth, but to empower individuals to make informed choices about their lives and families.

In conclusion, a rising share of children under 15 is neither inherently good nor bad. Its impact depends entirely on policy choices, resource allocation, and societal commitment. With foresight and sustained investment, these countries can turn youthful energy into lasting progress; without it, they risk squandering a profound opportunity—and paying a heavy price.

A lot of old people are suffering from loneliness these days. They also lack physical fitness. This dual challenge is not only a personal hardship but also a growing social concern. Several interconnected reasons contribute to this problem, and addressing them requires thoughtful, compassionate, and practical solutions.

One major reason is the changing family structure. In many societies, multigenerational households are becoming rare. Younger family members often move away for education or work, leaving elderly parents living alone. Without daily interaction, older adults gradually feel isolated. Another factor is retirement: after decades of professional engagement, suddenly losing routine, purpose, and social contact can lead to emotional withdrawal and reduced motivation to stay active. Additionally, declining health—such as hearing loss, mobility issues, or chronic illness—can make it difficult for seniors to participate in community life or even leave their homes. Technology gaps further widen the divide; many older people struggle to use smartphones or video-calling apps, cutting them off from virtual connections that others rely on.

To tackle loneliness and poor physical fitness, we need both individual and societal efforts. First, local communities can organize regular intergenerational activities—like shared gardening projects, storytelling sessions at schools, or tech-help workshops led by teenagers. These foster meaningful relationships while building mutual understanding. Second, governments and NGOs should expand accessible, low-cost exercise programs tailored for seniors, such as gentle tai chi classes in parks or home-based online fitness sessions with voice-guided instructions. Third, families can commit to consistent, quality time—not just occasional visits, but weekly phone calls, shared meals, or simple walks together. Even small gestures, like sending handwritten letters or involving elders in family decision-making, reinforce their sense of value and belonging.

Ultimately, combating loneliness and promoting physical well-being among the elderly is not just about solving a problem—it’s about honoring a lifetime of contributions and reaffirming that every person, at every age, deserves connection, dignity, and vitality.

Crime has long been a subject of debate among sociologists, psychologists, and policymakers. Some argue that criminal behavior stems primarily from external circumstances—such as poverty, lack of education, unemployment, and social inequality—while others contend that crime arises from inherent moral failings or biological predispositions. Both perspectives offer valuable insights, yet a balanced understanding reveals that neither view alone fully explains the complexity of human behavior.

Proponents of the environmental explanation point to strong correlations between socioeconomic disadvantage and crime rates. In underfunded neighborhoods, limited access to quality schooling, mental health services, and legitimate employment opportunities can push individuals toward illegal activities as a means of survival or expression of frustration. Studies show that communities with higher investment in social welfare, youth programs, and community policing often experience significant drops in crime—even without changes in law enforcement tactics. This suggests that altering conditions rather than focusing solely on punishment can yield lasting results.

On the other hand, those who emphasize individual responsibility highlight cases where people from privileged backgrounds commit serious crimes—white-collar fraud, abuse of power, or acts of violence without apparent external provocation. Psychological research also identifies traits such as low empathy, impulsivity, and poor self-regulation, which may have genetic or neurological roots. While environment shapes behavior, it does not entirely determine it; personal choices, values, and conscience still play crucial roles.

In my view, crime is rarely caused by either circumstance or nature alone—it emerges from their interaction. A person’s biology may influence temperament, but whether that temperament leads to crime depends heavily on upbringing, education, role models, and opportunity. Similarly, poverty increases risk, but not everyone in hardship turns to crime; resilience, support networks, and access to alternatives make critical differences. Therefore, effective crime prevention must combine structural reforms—like reducing inequality and improving public services—with efforts to foster ethical development, emotional intelligence, and accountability at the individual level.

Ultimately, framing the issue as “nature versus nurture” oversimplifies reality. What we need is compassion without excuse, justice without vengeance, and policy grounded in evidence—not ideology.

In many organizations, directors earn significantly higher salaries than ordinary workers, a practice that sparks ongoing debate. Some argue that such pay disparities are justified and necessary, while others consider them fundamentally unfair. Both perspectives have merit, and a balanced examination reveals the complexity of the issue.

Supporters of high executive pay contend that directors bear exceptional responsibility and pressure. They make strategic decisions that affect the entire organization—its financial health, reputation, and long-term survival. Their expertise, experience, and leadership skills are rare and in high demand globally; attracting and retaining top talent often requires competitive compensation packages. Moreover, performance-based bonuses and stock options aim to align directors’ interests with those of shareholders, encouraging decisions that foster growth and sustainability. From this viewpoint, high salaries are not rewards for status but investments in effective leadership.

On the other hand, critics argue that the widening pay gap undermines fairness, morale, and social cohesion. When a director earns hundreds of times more than a frontline employee—despite the latter’s dedication, skill, and indispensable contribution—it can breed resentment and weaken teamwork. Many ordinary workers face rising living costs, job insecurity, and limited opportunities for advancement, making extreme inequality feel unjust and demoralizing. Furthermore, evidence suggests that excessive executive pay does not always correlate with better company performance; sometimes, it even encourages short-term, risk-taking behavior at the expense of employees and long-term stability.

In my opinion, while directors deserve appropriate compensation for their responsibilities, the current scale of disparity is often excessive and unsustainable. A fairer system would cap executive pay relative to median worker wages—say, at a ratio of 20:1 or 30:1—and tie bonuses more transparently to measurable outcomes that benefit all stakeholders, including employees and communities. Organizations thrive not through isolated leadership brilliance, but through collaborative effort; recognizing and rewarding that collective contribution is essential for ethical, resilient, and truly successful enterprises.

Some people believe that scientists have more influence over the world, while others argue that politicians hold greater power in shaping global affairs. Both perspectives carry weight, and a closer examination reveals how each group exerts distinct yet deeply interconnected forms of influence.

On one hand, scientists drive progress in ways that fundamentally transform human life. Medical breakthroughs—such as vaccines, antibiotics, and gene-editing technologies—have saved billions of lives and extended average lifespans dramatically. Advances in information technology, renewable energy, and artificial intelligence are reshaping economies, communication, and daily existence. Scientific discoveries often transcend borders and ideologies; climate science, for instance, informs global policy regardless of national politics. Moreover, scientific knowledge accumulates gradually but irreversibly—once understood, it cannot be unlearned, giving science a lasting, cumulative impact on civilization.

On the other hand, politicians possess the authority to implement, regulate, and fund large-scale change. They draft laws, allocate resources, declare wars or peace, and negotiate international agreements. A single policy decision—such as banning fossil fuels, legalizing same-sex marriage, or launching a national education reform—can alter the course of millions of lives overnight. Unlike scientific ideas, which require time and infrastructure to take effect, political decisions can produce immediate, tangible consequences. Furthermore, politicians control the conditions under which science operates: they decide research budgets, ethical boundaries, and whether scientific advice is heeded—or ignored—in times of crisis.

In my view, while scientists generate the knowledge and tools that make progress possible, politicians determine how—and whether—that progress serves humanity. Their influence is not equal in kind, but complementary. Scientists illuminate the path; politicians choose the direction and build the road. However, in today’s complex world, where misinformation spreads rapidly and urgent challenges like climate change and pandemics demand coordinated action, the most influential figures are often those who bridge both domains—leaders who value evidence and scientists who engage thoughtfully with policy. Ultimately, lasting global influence arises not from science alone or politics alone, but from their responsible and respectful collaboration.

Housing is indeed a fundamental human need, essential for dignity, health, and social stability. While the idea of providing free housing to all who cannot afford it sounds compassionate and just, I believe this policy is neither practically feasible nor the most effective solution. I partially disagree with the statement, as it oversimplifies a complex socioeconomic issue.

On one hand, the moral argument for free housing is compelling. Without safe and stable shelter, individuals struggle to secure employment, access education, or maintain physical and mental well-being. Homelessness exacerbates inequality and places long-term burdens on healthcare and law enforcement systems. In this sense, governments do bear a responsibility to ensure housing security—especially for the most vulnerable, such as low-income families, the elderly, and people with disabilities.

However, declaring housing “free for everyone who can’t afford it” ignores critical realities. First, the financial cost would be enormous. Building, maintaining, and managing sufficient high-quality housing for all financially excluded citizens would require unprecedented public expenditure—likely at the expense of other vital services like education, healthcare, or infrastructure. Second, blanket “free” provision risks inefficiency and unintended consequences: it may reduce incentives for personal financial planning, discourage private-sector investment in affordable housing, or lead to misallocation of resources if eligibility criteria are poorly designed or enforced.

A more balanced and sustainable approach involves targeted support rather than universal provision. Governments can expand subsidized housing, offer rent vouchers tied to income, implement progressive taxation to fund housing programs, and regulate markets to prevent speculation and price inflation. Equally important is addressing root causes—such as wage stagnation, unemployment, and lack of skills training—so that more people can achieve housing affordability through economic participation.

In conclusion, while the government must play a strong, proactive role in guaranteeing housing access for those in genuine need, offering completely free housing to all who cannot afford it is neither realistic nor optimal. A smarter strategy combines immediate relief with long-term structural reforms—ensuring both compassion and sustainability.

The shortage of housing in big cities is a pressing global issue, leading to skyrocketing rents, overcrowded living conditions, and growing social inequality. While some argue that only government action can resolve this crisis, I believe that although governmental intervention is essential and irreplaceable, sustainable solutions also require coordinated efforts from the private sector, local communities, and individuals.

Undeniably, governments possess the unique authority and resources to address systemic housing shortages. They control land use planning, zoning regulations, and public funding—tools critical for large-scale affordable housing development. For instance, Singapore’s Housing and Development Board has successfully provided over 80% of its population with high-quality, subsidized flats through long-term policy commitment and strategic land allocation. Similarly, Vienna’s century-old social housing model—funded by progressive taxation and managed by the city—demonstrates how strong public leadership can ensure housing as a social right rather than a commodity. Without such top-down regulation and investment, market forces alone tend to prioritize profit over accessibility, exacerbating exclusion and segregation.

However, relying solely on governments is neither realistic nor sufficient. Bureaucratic delays, political short-termism, and budget constraints often hinder timely and adaptive responses. Moreover, innovative financing, construction technologies, and community-led housing initiatives—such as co-operatives or modular housing startups—thrive outside traditional state frameworks. In Tokyo, for example, private developers have responded to land scarcity with compact, efficiently designed micro-apartments, while grassroots organizations in Berlin have campaigned successfully for rent caps and tenant protections, influencing policy from below. These examples show that effective housing strategies emerge from synergy—not singularity.

In conclusion, while government action is the cornerstone of any meaningful solution to urban housing shortages, it must be complemented by private innovation, civic engagement, and inclusive urban planning. The challenge is too complex and multifaceted to be solved by one actor alone. What we need is not exclusivity of responsibility, but shared accountability—and a renewed collective commitment to making cities livable for all.

The idea that the traditional classroom system—featuring physical classrooms, face-to-face teaching, and fixed student-teacher roles—will vanish entirely by 2050 is both provocative and widely debated. While technological advancements are undeniably transforming education, I believe that the core human elements of teaching and learning will persist, even if their forms evolve significantly. Therefore, I only partially agree with the claim.

On one hand, compelling evidence supports the prediction of radical change. Artificial intelligence tutors, immersive virtual reality classrooms, and personalized adaptive learning platforms are already reshaping how knowledge is delivered and absorbed. Students in remote or underserved areas can now access world-class instruction online; AI can assess work instantly and tailor feedback to individual needs. In such a landscape, the conventional model—where one teacher lectures to thirty students in a fixed timetable—may indeed become outdated in many contexts. Moreover, lifelong learning trends suggest that formal schooling may no longer be confined to childhood, further blurring the lines between teacher, learner, and mentor.

However, education is far more than information transfer. It is about inspiration, empathy, ethical reasoning, and social development—dimensions that algorithms cannot replicate. A skilled teacher reads subtle cues: a student’s hesitation before answering, the quiet frustration behind a shrug, the spark of curiosity ignited by a well-timed question. These moments rely on presence, intuition, and relational trust—qualities no software can authentically simulate. Likewise, peer interaction in shared physical or synchronous digital spaces fosters collaboration, debate, and emotional intelligence in ways isolated, self-paced learning cannot fully replace.

By 2050, classrooms may look vastly different—hybrid, flexible, technology-rich—but teachers will not disappear; they will transform. They may become facilitators, coaches, or designers of learning experiences rather than sole knowledge authorities. Students, too, will take greater ownership of their learning, yet still need guidance, encouragement, and moral grounding from caring adults.

In conclusion, while the *form* of teaching and learning will undoubtedly evolve beyond recognition by 2050, the essential human relationship at its heart—the dynamic, responsive, and compassionate exchange between educator and learner—will endure. The classroom may change, but it will not vanish.

Some people believe that studying at university or college is the best route to a successful career, while others argue that entering the workforce immediately after school offers greater advantages. Both perspectives have merit, and the choice often depends on individual goals, circumstances, and the field of interest.

Supporters of higher education emphasize its long-term benefits. A university degree not only provides in-depth knowledge and critical thinking skills but also serves as a widely recognized credential that opens doors to many professional opportunities—especially in fields such as medicine, law, engineering, and academia. Moreover, university life fosters personal growth through exposure to diverse ideas, independent learning, and networking with peers and professors. Many employers still view a degree as evidence of commitment, discipline, and intellectual capability.

On the other hand, those who favour starting work right after school highlight practical experience, financial independence, and early career progression. Apprenticeships, vocational training, and entry-level jobs allow individuals to develop hands-on skills, build a professional portfolio, and earn income without accumulating student debt. In fast-evolving industries like digital marketing, software development, or creative design, real-world projects and demonstrable competence often matter more than formal qualifications. Furthermore, some young people thrive in dynamic, action-oriented environments rather than academic ones—and delaying or forgoing university may better suit their learning style and ambitions.

In my opinion, there is no universal “best” path. While university remains invaluable for certain careers and personal aspirations, it is not the only route to success. What truly matters is alignment: choosing the path that matches one’s interests, strengths, and long-term vision. With increasing recognition of alternative credentials—such as certifications, online courses, and portfolio-based assessments—the definition of a successful career is becoming more flexible and inclusive. Ultimately, lifelong learning and adaptability matter far more than the timing or format of one’s initial education.

Education is often seen as the cornerstone of personal development and national progress. While it is true that governments worldwide invest heavily in the education of children and adolescents, I firmly believe that prioritizing literacy and basic education for illiterate adults deserves even greater attention and financial commitment. I strongly agree that governments should allocate more resources to adult education programs.

Firstly, adult illiteracy represents a profound barrier to social inclusion and economic participation. Millions of adults—many of whom are parents, workers, or community members—struggle daily with tasks most of us take for granted: reading medicine labels, filling out job applications, understanding contracts, or helping their children with homework. Without basic literacy, they remain marginalized, vulnerable to exploitation, and unable to exercise full citizenship. Investing in their education is not merely charitable; it is an investment in societal stability, public health, and long-term economic productivity.

Secondly, educating adults yields immediate and tangible benefits that ripple across generations.
